moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, a flexible system that controls the center from the side rather than occupying it immediately. The c4 pawn eyes d5 and e6, giving us strategic control while keeping our position adaptable to Black's setup. This move is particularly useful because it doesn't commit us to a fixed pawn structure, letting us react to Black's plans—exactly what makes the English so popular at the highest levels.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with this Dutch Defense-style setup, attacking e4 and preparing to support the center with natural pieces. However, f5 weakens the e6 square and the long diagonal, which we should keep in mind as we develop. This move tells us Black wants to control space and create early counterplay rather than play defensively.

We develop naturally while keeping multiple options open—the knight controls e5 and d4, preventing Black from easily supporting a strong center. Nf3 is incredibly flexible; it fits into virtually any plan we choose next (whether we push e4, play d4, or even maintain tension). We're not rushing to commit; we're developing with purpose and leaving Black to show us their full intentions.

Black reinforces their control of e5 and prepares to support a pawn advance or reroute pieces. The move also prepares a potential ...e5 advance or allows Black flexibility with their light-squared bishop. By playing d6, Black is being cautious and keeping their options open, building a solid but somewhat passive setup.

Now we strike at the center with purpose, taking advantage of the weakened e6 and long diagonal created by Black's f5 and d6. The e4 push is the Chabanon Gambit idea—we're prepared to accept ...fxe4 and use our space advantage and development lead to attack. This move transforms the position into something concrete: we have a strong pawn center and Black must decide whether to capture or allow e5, either way giving us the initiative we've been setting up.
